entry.title: Alpha Complete!
entry.content:
The first phase of Launch Academy has come to a close, and I’m supposed to be reflecting on a specific concept that I found difficult. To be honest, the greatest challenge over these past two weeks has been managing time and energy effectively. With our work weeks exceeding 70 hours, trying to find a good balance between work, sleep, excercise, and time to unwind has been difficult. When I’ve compromised on any one of those factors, the quality and pace at which I learn and work is appreciably affected. As I enter the second phase, here are some lessons I have learned about how I work best.

Excercise: Other than actually coding itself, this is the single greatest factor in how efficient and alert I remain through the day. Our days at Launch Academy are long, 12 hours are often required to get the most out of the day. A good workout in the morning is the key to staying mentally alert, and physically tired enough to fall asleep early and get a good night’s sleep. The days I skimp on this I suffer.

Pairing: I was extremely resistant to the idea of pairing as I felt it slowed me down. This is especially relevant since the amount of time we have to spend just to keep up means any time wasted could have us falling behind. My opinion on pairing remained pretty constant until we approached the end of the phase. As our projects grew bigger and started to span over multiple days having a partner to help organize, troubleshoot, and just keep motivation up in general had tons of value. I can definitely see where it will matter as we start Rails and have assignments with longer timelines.

Planning: According to some of my peers, I have a tendency to just “spaz” code out when solving a problem. This was fine in the early part of the phase when our problems were simple and our code short, but as our assignments have grown more elaborate this methodology (or lack of) comes with a lot of technical debt. Once again, I found myself initially resistant to whiteboarding and planning but now realize it’s essential to plan out how you’re modelling your data before you begin. Looking forward to learn databases and ActiveRecord so I never have to store data in a .csv again.

We’ll see what phase 2 brings, Launch Academy is forcing me to start picking up good habits that will stay with me after the program is done in eight weeks (that soon!).

entry.title: The Breakable Toy
entry.content:
Today I began working on one of the most interesting parts of Launch Academy, the breakable toy. It’s an application of our own choosing that we will build, then break and rebuild as part of the program. The idea is to have a place where we can put the concepts we’re learning into a real project. It’s our first exposure to full-stack programming and the design and development of software.

In choosing my breakable toy I wanted to build something that solved a problem that hadn’t been solved with software. I decided to build a program that matches patients on a standby list (to see a doctor/dentist) with cancelled appointments they are available for. I wanted something simple so I don’t want to integrate/interfere with current scheduling systems. I’ve worked in a doctor’s office before and from talking to some in the industry cancellations and double bookings are a big problem this may help alleviate.

The program is quite straightforward. The patient availability will be entered into the system. When there is a cancellation, it will be entered into the system. They system will rank the available patients for that timeslot, and the first to confirm will be assigned that appointment time.

There are 2 components to the application I feel will be the most challenging. The matching system needs to be able to message multiple users to make sure that it fills the spot quickly, but in a way that gives users time to respond and doesn’t alienate those that don’t respond quickly. Secondly, the users will most likely be sent an email link to confirm, but ideally they could do so by SMS or email directly.

Right now I’ve only begun writing user stories, but the breakable toy is a great way to go through the development cycle from planning to deployment in the time we have here at Launch Academy.

entry.title: Launch Academy: Week 1 Synopsis
entry.content:
The first week at Launch Academy has come to a close, and I know I’ve learned a lot because I begin the second week with more questions than I had coming in. I started the course with some basic ruby knowledge, in one week I’m ready to tackle some tougher problems and start writing more advanced ruby in a object oriented way. The instructors have been great, the talks have really helped give context to a lot of stuff I had tried to learn myself and didn’t really understand; and I’m getting comfortable navigating my development environment with the command line, sublime text, and pry. I find myself learning an order of magnitude faster because there’s always someone to help explain a concept when I get stuck and the energy level and motivation of my peers keeps me going.

What really struck me about my Launch Academy experience is how much I enjoyed it despite the intensity and hours required to keep up. I’ve always loved learning and have had to cram for exams before, but this is nothing like those University finals weeks. Firstly, I’m not graded or ranked, so rather than gaming the system to try and get a passing mark the only thing that matters is the quality of my code. This fundamental shift changes the motivation for hard work and success from extrinsic to intrinsic. I find myself putting in 12+ hour days and rather than feeling burned out it has positive effects on the other aspects of my daily life. Even with a 70 hour plus workweek I find myself at the gym more often and waking up earlier. Doesn’t hurt that everyone else in the room is having the same great experience.

The collaborative atmosphere in which work gets done is the icing on the cake. People often avoid working with computers because they feel they aren’t suited to sit in front of a computer all day. That isn’t what it’s like at all. After having worked on a construction site, mine, factory, restaurant, office, and car wash; I can definitely say that if working as a developer is anything like Launch Academy, it is one of the best work environments that exists. After attending a Boston Ruby meetup this week and Dan’s talk on Agile development I realize this is not something that will end in 10 weeks but will be part of the culture at many of the companies in this industry.

I head into the second week looking forward to improving my object oriented Ruby skills, and seeing what else Dan and the team teach me about the industry and the subject matter. It’s only been a week but I definitely feel I’ve come to the right place.

entry.title: Why I'm at Launch Academy
entry.content:
I had been frustrated with University, after working multiple internships and switching majors I found myself still unsatisfied with the path I was on and not motivated academically. Finally, in the summer of 2011 I was looking to do something different and I left for India. I spend the summer building a computer lab from scratch amidst monsoons, extreme heat, and frequent power outages. I taught the students to build computers, use them, and access the internet for the first time. I taught some english and math using Khan Academy and the philosophy of the “flipped classroom” to great effect. The summer was transformational, and I unearthed a passion for technology, creative problem solving, and teaching.

The following year, some friends and I decided we wanted to start a business, and our idea happened to be software based. I felt the intensity and problems solving inherent in starting a business would best replicate the sort of experience I had in India. None of us had a software background but we felt being in a university town we’d easily find developers if our idea was good. To make a very long story short, it was incredibly difficult to find/retain talent with no funds, and repeatedly delaying simple tasks like a decent landing page left me and my co founder frustrated enough to start learning HTML and CSS on our own. I touched upon Ruby and Rails from some other resources and I was hooked.

I’m at Launch Academy because it will help me become a great junior Ruby on Rails Developer. Even more importantly, it puts me on a path where creativity, problem solving, technology, and learning or teaching new things are part of the job description.
